topic: Crafting Strong Introductions and Planning Arguments
title: Making Your Arguments Start Smart

questions.question: What does "rhetorical question" mean?
options: a question asked to make a point or make someone think, rather than to get an answer
questions.correct_answer: a question asked to make a point or make someone think, rather than to get an answer
questions.explanation: "rhetorical question" means: a question asked to make a point or make someone think, rather than to get an answer

questions.question: Fill in the blank: Use rhetorical questions to make your ______ interesting and thought-provoking.
questions.explanation: The full sentence is: "Use rhetorical questions to make your introduction interesting and thought-provoking."

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about this topic?
options: Plan your arguments by listing points 'for' and 'against' your topic.
Use rhetorical questions to make your introduction interesting and thought-provoking.
Ensure your introduction clearly states what the argument will cover.
It has no special meaning in writing.
questions.correct_answer: Plan your arguments by listing points 'for' and 'against' your topic.
questions.explanation: Correct! "Plan your arguments by listing points 'for' and 'against' your topic." is one of the key things to remember.

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about this topic?
options: Use rhetorical questions to make your introduction interesting and thought-provoking.
Plan your arguments by listing points 'for' and 'against' your topic.
Ensure your introduction clearly states what the argument will cover.
It has no special meaning in writing.
questions.correct_answer: Ensure your introduction clearly states what the argument will cover.
questions.explanation: Correct! "Ensure your introduction clearly states what the argument will cover." is one of the key things to remember.
